comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Cyril Scott, who was both a lifelong Theosophist and occultist and a brilliant Romantic composer born at the wrong time. His more than 400 musical works were unfashionable during the heyday of atonal music, and at his death in 1970 he was widely ignored in the musical world (though his music has had a major revival since 2000); his books on occultism and alternative medicine were more enthusiastically received within the occult subculture, and The Initiate and its two sequels were practically required reading in the scene when I first got involved in it. (Here's Scott playing "Lotus Land" on the piano.)

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. 

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

replies.content:
1) Not occult, but based on experience I would suggest looking at psychology material that relates to your personal background and traits, and seeing what chimes. That may move things forward, both in terms of thinking about what's behind the excessive internet use, and in making you consider other issues.

Are you already trying practical methods to limit internet use, such as timers, or notification alerts that give you permission to look at a certain thing for a limited duration at a specified time?

There are often two strands with these things: underlying psychological reasons AND being in a comfortable groove with the habitual behaviour. You need to address both.

There is also the matter of what else it is stopping you doing. Make lists of other things you need to do, and things you want to do that don't involve the web, and do them before you go online that morning, or before you fire up the computer after getting home. Put off starting to use the internet as long as possible. For essential online tasks such as banking, do them before any leisure internet use.

There is a huge amount of stuff out there on the internet that is really interesting. But you can't look at it all. You have to say no even to a lot of material that is good in itself, and prioritise what is actually important.

It's an exercise in willpower, and willpower is important to occult work: you could see it as training for that.

5) Again a less occult approach: read up about how sleep is structured and its different phases. Remembering dreams just means you woke up in a certain phase. If you didn't, it can mean you are better rested.
If you are sleep deprived it can be harder to work on other stuff, and to process things mentally. Don't deprive yourself of sleep for no good reason. Better sleep might even help you work out more about the internet overuse, and improve your interest in other activities.

replies.title: dream work
replies.content:
If I may, I've studied dreamwork and I have a few suggestions.
1. Affirming that you remember your dreams is good. The second thing to do is to write them down, even if all you remember is that you had a dream. Or if you just have an impression of a color, or an emotion. Write it down. The more you pay attention to your dreams, the more you'll begin to remember. Eventually, it can be almost like a conversation between you and the dream world, but the first step is to show the dream world that you're serious by writing down even the least little impression of what you remember.
2. You might want to add an affirmation that "It's safe for me to remember my dreams" or "remembering my dreams is safe and fun" or something like that. I say that because it sounds like you might have some anxiety around that issue.
3. As for books, I always recommend Dreamwork by Jeremy Taylor, although that book is really oriented toward group work. But the techniques can be used solo. I also enjoyed The Art of Dreaming by Jill Mellick. Taylor's techniques are more or less discursive, whereas Mellick goes at dreams from an artist perspective -- kind of an opposite approach. Taylor has you looking methodically at each element of your dream, where Mellick's techniques are more like playing with the dream, turning it around and looking at it from different angles, which produces a deeper understanding.
4. Know that you'll probably never get "the one true interpretation" of any dream. Because such a thing doesn't exist. Dreams have so many levels that even a simple dream can produce more than one "true" interpretation. The point of dreamwork is deeper understanding, not a one-to-one decoding of the dream. There's no Secret Decoder Ring in dreamwork.
5. I believe it's ok to use dream dictionaries, especially if you're working solo, but the best way to approach them is as if the book were a friend and you're just bouncing the dream off him or her. Think of the book as saying "if this were my dream, the tree would mean X." Do not approach the dictionary as if it had the Real True Meanings of each dream symbol. Any dictionary is just one writer's perspective, not some ultimate truth.
I hope that's helpful and I hope you have a lot of fun and insight during your dreamwork adventures.

Re: 1b: Given personal experience and the reports of others, I'd be surprised if there aren't at least four broad magical regions on the continent, and I suspect at least seven (roughly Avalonia, Lakeland, Canadian Shield, Great Plains, Mesoamerica, and Cascadia, plus the Coastal Plain south of Avalonia). One of the big question marks to me is whether there's actually a distinction between Mesoamerica and the Great Plains; my impression is that there's a terminator roughly in the vicinity of the Balcones Fault, but I'm not sure about that, and there are similarities between what I brushed and what I've read about Mesoamerica. (Given a few things I've read about the nature of Mesoamerican religious/sports festivals - which strike me as quite similar to Southern football culture, which leaves me utterly cold - plus personal mythos and my track record of reinventing occult concepts from scratch, it occurs to me that the actual situation might be a split a level up from the underlying pattern, vaguely reminiscent of the asura/deva split in Iran and India. Admittedly Iran/India isn't the best analogy - Iran and India are both sources of very different deep cultures, with Iran as the Magian source opposed to India's cyclic time, whereas if this speculation is correct the Great Plains and Mesoamerica would be two opposed halves of a single deep culture - but it seems somewhat more appropriate than anything else I can think of.) Either way, I'm pretty sure that the Great Plains stop short of the Arctic, and I'm quite confident that said region pushes towards a fractal view of spacetime - though I'm still working on parsing the implications of that.

On a small scale, large chunks of Mississippi and Monroe, LA seem to be a spiritual dead zone comparable to a death row town (and I mean that quite literally - I went to an overnight event in Huntsville, TX once). Biloxi's problem was probably the Gulf dead zone, but I'm not sure about the I-20 corridor; my best guess is aftereffects of slavery/segregation, given that I-20 is in the Black Belt. (I doubt it's just the Mississippi/future ocean rise, Baton Rouge seemed unaffected.) Meanwhile, my loose impression is that there's something slumbering under the Piney Woods of eastern Texas that's slightly different from the energies near Dallas, and that it meshes poorly with the Southern culture currently built on top of it.

Also, while I've never been up to the Great Lakes, I've read things from other people that suggest that Lake Superior may be a very large example of the "eating places" Robert Mathiesen (sp?) sometimes talks about.

- pretentious_username

comments.title: Non-material mess
comments.content:
From what I can gather, nobody seems to know much about the astrology of the fungal kingdom, so I've been experimenting. On this last full moon, while she was still on the waxing side, I racked my 5-gallon carboy of mead (made in a 3rd-quarter Virgo moon in January and fermenting very slowly, as one might expect for such timing) over a rotting mango that smelled absolutely delicious. Like the best wine you've ever rolled through your nostrils; I literally couldn't help myself.

This must have been the driving force for what happened that night; I can't come up with anything else that could've sent our house "spirit" into such a tirade. I hope I didn't ruin the mead.

It wasn't a quiet night. Full moons can be that way of course, but it was an especially active night, even though the dogs were completely silent. Naturally we blamed it on the cat...

What we found when we woke up was pretty wild though. First thing I noticed was the burner I make coffee on every morning: as I lit it it flamed up high, as if the gas orifice had been changed to the natural gas setting and we were burning propane. We weren't of course. I had to turn it down below 4 to get the flame low enough to hit the kettle, when I normally set it to 5. Strange but not a big deal.

Next I went to get milk for said coffee but couldn't find it in the fridge. I knew we had some though, and eventually found it in the pantry - still cool, as if it had been there for only a couple hours, not room temp yet. Well now this is starting to get odd.

But then I took the coffee into the living room and there in the middle of the floor was a ball of yarn my wife had just bought and prepped to make a bathmat with. There were two identical balls in her knitting basket when we went to bed; one of them was still intact in the basket, the other strewn about the floor in one giant knot.

Cat right? Um...no. This was a mess that no animal could have made. We didn't really realize just how dastardly a tangle it was until my wife started trying to untangle it a few days ago. She spent her entire knitting time for 3 days working through this scramble. It was knotted in ways that are impossible. Knots tied in the middle where there was no way to make that work, fibers crossing and entangled with each other in impossible ways, and...yarn from different parts of the ball with fibers spun TOGETHER - literally run through each other, impossible to separate without damaging the yarn.

The only explanation that makes any sense to us, if that's what you call it, is that the yarn was taken into a non-material plane and scrambled about thoroughly, then brought back into the material plane. There is literally no way ANYONE, even with opposable thumbs, could've done what was done to that yarn in the physical plane.

Quite a night! Any thoughts? Have you or anyone else here had similar experiences? Certainly a new one for me...

Tripp

replies.title: Re: Non-material mess
replies.content: Impressive but not unheard of -- poltergeist phenomena on that scale have been documented. You probably need to figure out what to do to get the house spirit less peeved at you, though.

replies.title: Re: Non-material mess
replies.content:
OK. Feels like things have settled back down somewhat at this point. The burner on the stove stayed high for a week or so before going back to normal. We made an offering of milk in the spot we associate with our "spirit," and have repeated that several times since. The mead smells good and is bubbling faster than before. The milk has stayed in the fridge since then.

What else do you suggest doing? General tidying, cleaning, power-washing the house? Keep going with the floor leveling we talked about earlier? Are there any ritual "offerings" we could make? Another hoodoo floor wash perhaps?

Thank you!!
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Non-material mess
replies.content:
When my husband and I moved into our house two years ago, I began an everyday routine of feeding and watering the various animals with birdseed, cat food, and a ground-level birdbath that is heated in winter. I didn't think much of it. One day, I was changing the seed in the bird feeders and the S hook that holds the feeder to the pole fell under our rickety front steps underneath deep snow. Man, was that a frustrating moment. I looked for a few minutes but I knew I wouldn't find it. It was after work and I was dead tired, so I said "oh eff this" and I got some craft wire and rope in place of the missing hook and put the feeder up in that sorry state. The next day, I was about to get in my car, which of course had been locked all night (we park on the street or in the back alley) and the S hook was sitting right in the middle of the driver's seat.

I knew it was the land spirits thanking me for feeding/watering everybody.

replies.title: Re: Non-material mess
replies.content:
That is seriously cool. Did you catch my story about my wife's favorite necklace disappearing and then coming back to her after she showed mercy to a breed of dog she hates? I told that story a year or two ago in an open post at Ecosophia (iirc).

But thanks for YOUR story! Some of these things simply have no other explanation...although as recently as two years ago I still wouldn't have accepted it.

Been a good two years...

Tripp

replies.content:

replies.content:

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Last week I was thinking about what seem to be limitations in magic. First, there are plenty of spectacular failures, gruesomely so when people have tried to make themselves immune to bullets (tales of Boxer Rebellion aren't pretty). Second, there have been attempts to test psychic powers in laboratory conditions, and as a rule, the more rigorously controlled the test was, the less likely it was to find anything. Some of those were run by people who wanted to prove the reality of powers, so that can't be the issue. I don't know if anyone tested magic as such, but it seems at least plausible that the results would be similar.

Now, bullets have their trajectory determined to a very high degree when they are fired. The changes to that are also linear, so a little gust of wind will change it only a little, and actually throwing it completely off course or stopping it would require quite a bit of force. Most other systems in this world are non-linear, so a tiny change now can result in big effects later (the classic butterfly effect, basically). Calculating these changes is a complete pain in the fundament, and in any case impossible in the long term without perfectly accurate data, but they too operate according to rules. It's possible to postulate non-mathematical ways of understanding them, or superintelligences capable of getting close to perfect data and crunching the numbers.

As for scientific experiments, an ideal one controls for as many variables as possible, so it can look at what happens when you change the one being tested. If extrasensory perception and other such things operate through these variables instead of being independent of them, and the experimenter uses his will to keep them as stable as possible, grounding this in the physical setup of the experiment, it seems logical that the effect would vanish.

I'm really just spitballing here, but might we be dealing with something that operates through small, unobserved changes in chaotic systems with a lot of unknown variables? I could also go "argle gargle collapse of wave function when observed argle", but my eyes start rolling uncontrollably when people try to give magic a pseudoscientific veneer, and while I didn't just slap a quantum sticker on it, I went close enough already.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If I may... I have studied and worked extensively with defense magic (shields, blessings, amulets, banishings). Magic, like everything else has limits, but usually (and this is MY opinion, based on my experience) people force defense magic to fail because it is often too successful. Let me explain, lets supposed that someone is targeting you and you make a protective amulet. The amulet will not stop a bullet, but it can cause a small twitch of the wrist, a headache, a gush of wind that throws dust into the gunslinger's eyes, a pigeon to fly in front of the bullet, a bee to sting him/her.If they're using a sniper rifle and there's even a minor error in adjustment the bullet can fail. The amulet can end up  being so successful that you convince yourself that you're invulnerable and start taking more risks. There's always a limit. You spoke of the Boxer rebellion. That is a classic case of too much success. The Boxer rebel usually had a spell to make sure that whoever shot at him wouldn't be able to hit him. That spell would probably be successful at mid-range and with one, perhaps two, at the most three shooters, and that is already a stretch. Now imagine three thousand men, cramped in a Beijing street. The person that is shooting at them may fail, but the bullet for each one of them will probably hit the guy on their side and vice versa. Not nice.On the other hand we have a much more successful example of protective magic recently. During the Vietnam war, the Tiger battalion from Korea was deployed in Vietnam. A significant percentage of the members of that batallion had magical protection tattoos that were supposed to make them invulnerable. All of them returned home without any injuries... How was that possible? For one reason or another the batallion was always barred from engaging the "enemy". They spent all the war in the barracks.

comments.title: Banishing
comments.content:
Questions about what specifically to banish.

1) I might have worked out my own answers to a couple of things on the subject of banishing negatives, while writing the questions. Would like to run these by you and others anyway. Banishing debt? I think this might be fine, as it's useful to cultivate an attitude that debt is something to avoid, but it should also be supplemented by positive workings for money and opportunities. However banishing an illness rather than positive working for good health seems like it might be a bad idea, because firstly being ill involves being alive, and one way for it to stop might be to not be alive, and secondly the issue of bad side effects as found with some medicines.
What else is there to watch out for in the idea of banishing negatives?

2) Banishings that involve things that people do and say. How does this work with the ethics against magic to control people? Whether one is banishing a person generally? Or if they are less feasible to cease contact with totally, but the goal is to hear from them less, or that they behave differently, where the usual requests and discussion made little impact?

replies.title: Re: Banishing
replies.content:
1) Magic is the art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will, and you'll have more success banishing things the closer they are to consciousness. Banishing debt rarely works, because debt is an effect, not a cause -- it's an effect of spending more than your income will permit. Banish carelessness, wastefulness, and the attitude of entitlement that says you should have something whether or not you can afford it, and debt will go away promptly. In the same way, don't banish illnesses, banish states of consciousness that encourage you to get sick.

2) Here again, don't banish the person, banish your reaction to the person. Banish your vulnerability to their games; banish the emotional triggers they exploit. Change your consciousness, and they'll go away once they no longer get what they want.

comments.title: Composer considering a simple working
comments.content:

Furthermore this project was "shiny" in that its subject matter involved famous and appealing names that everyone would know, including you JMG. (I will not give more specifics so as not to run afoul of "no advertising" rules, as well as to keep my anonymity.) So I've been rather dismayed that my album has sunk like a stone. Perhaps this is where "fate" enters the equation, closing the door that destiny opened. Despite the shiny subject matter and the really (in my own unabashedly biased opinion) excellent and enjoyable qualities of the album, not even a single review in even a minor music blog has appeared anywhere. Let alone in a Big Important Mainstream Media outlet. There are precisely 3 reviews on the Amazon page listing my music, and they are all friends of mine or family of someone who helped make the album. It's quite sad, really. This is after both the record label hired a PR company, and (after things were too quiet) I went and hired another on my own as well. I am continuing to try other avenues to introduce it to people, but no biters yet.

So if destiny and fate have both had their say, then, "will" is the next logical step. Taking the cue of the timing of your recent Magic Monday "composer/occultist" theme, I started thinking... what if I attempt a magical working to help get my music heard by a wider audience? I am not a practicing occultist, and the watcher at the threshold has always managed to get me before I ever got very far in any structured occult training. However I have experimented (rarely) with doing workings a couple of times and been lucky enough get overall positive results. (I once did a justice working before I understood anything about raspberry jam. Whooo-ee did I feel the blowback on that one... but it seemed to work, and the still-ongoing blowback was I believe good for me at the end of the day.) So, pursuing this line of thought for a moment...

(1) You always warn to do divination before deciding to go ahead with a working. I've been a casual tarot user for a few years now, so I did some quick draws. The initial questions I asked-- "What's the overal result if I do a working to have this music reach the ears of a wider audience?" comments.title: Dreaming
comments.content:
Mr Greer, I had a new experience for me with my dreams and wanted to share it and see if you had some recommended reading where I might learn more.

Some background first. I had been stressing out about a job change (specifically, the contract company I work for will be changing to one that could mean a huge loss of benefits and potentially pay) and asked for guidance. At that time, I did not know what the offer was going to be, but based on past experience, it did not look promising. I had been applying for other jobs, and debating whether I should continue to do so or wait and stay with the new company. I had also learned that my 16 year old cat would need to be put down, and was feeling absolutely horrible about it.

Now, I do not normally remember my dreams, even a little. But this night I had a dream that I can still remember decently well. It was cloaked in all the metaphor that a dream is, but there was a very clear moment after the metaphoric part, where (1) I realized I was in a dream, (2) realized there was meaning to, and then (3) intuitively knew what the message behind it was. "Do not fear the unknown," was the message.

I wasn't really sure what to make of it at the time, but it did give me a little solace about losing my pet. About a week later, I received a phone call from the contract company, and the salary they offered me was a lot more than I was expecting, making up for the lost benefits. I feel pretty certain that this was what the dream's message was about.

I then had another dream, similar to the first in that I realized I was dreaming and intuited the message from it. "Do not take your wife for granted," was the new message. My wife and I have a pretty wonderful marriage, but even so this seemed like sage life advice, and I have been doing what I can to be extra helpful with the kids and around the house.

I guess my question is, how do I go about figuring out what I tapped into here? I haven't had any more dreams like the two above since. What should be on my recommended reading list along these lines? Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Dreaming
replies.content: Fascinating. Dreamwork isn't something I've studied, but since you're getting good advice, I'd encourage you to listen to it!

comments.title: A spot of magical frustration
comments.content:
I've been practicing magic for several years. My regular practice is based on Golden Dawn structures from "Circles of Power," adapted to Hellenic deities. I regularly practice the LBRP with circulation of the light, the invocation of the Sun, and occasionally my Hellenic answer to the Rose Cross. I often engage in Tarot divination in a fairly unsophisticated way but seem to get decent results. Recently I've been practicing the meditation from Tony Mierzwicki's "Graeco-Egyptian Magick," which involves open-vowel chanting and visualization of the planetary spheres.

My principal desire is to improve my material situation, which like that of many Americans increasingly sucks. And this is where I seem stuck. I've asked the gods for help and guidance. I've done rituals and made offerings to them. I've read their hymns for years. I've followed Israel Regardie's recommendations from "The Art of True Healing." I've engaged in some pretty intensive prayer. I think I may have helped myself and family temporarily avoid catastrophe on one or two occasions; but nothing has brought me the kind of opportunities I need to get out of my rut. I feel like I've been rattling in the box but never popping out. So I'm wondering if there's a way that I can focus my attention and activities to figure out what I'm omitting and need to start doing, both magically and materially.

Last year I did a *thorough* delineation of my horoscope using Llewellyn George as my reference (not the good version - the library ILL never sends me that). It tells me that I'm pretty much guaranteed to fail at what I love (art) more or less forever, or until I have a foot in the grave (not so far off now). It also tells me I have a chance of success at things I never dreamed of: institutions and public affairs; literature, books, publishing, occultism(?!), religious and educational affairs, short journeys, foreigners and inventions, to name some of the more prominent examples.

The problem is I have no idea where to put my shoulder to the wheel. Most of the institutions around here are corrupt; the only one I respect is the public library, and I do not have a degree in library science. How to get started with the others, where to put out feelers and start talking to people? I'm really at sea here.

Another factor is the psychological one. For decades I've made the *utmost* efforts to succeed in what I love, and Fortuna's answer has been to plant her stiletto heels on my face for forty years. This is not a confidence builder. It tends to produce a belief, very difficult to uproot, that all one's efforts, however great, are guaranteed to be in vain. So that's a rock I have to somehow get out of the way.

Any constructive suggestions you might happen to have would be very much appreciated.

By the way, thanks for doing these Magic Mondays. Your advice and informative observations are invaluable.

replies.title: Re: A spot of magical frustration
replies.content:
Okay, two things. First, repeated failure is a common experience for artists. Go read some biographies of artists and find out how many of them had financially successful lives. Especially in the modern era, when artists are taught to follow their own creative vision and ignore what their audiences might want to pay for, that's standard. It's basically what you signed on for when you became an artist.

Second, if you want to improve your material situation through magic, you're barking up the wrong stump. Unless I'm greatly mistaken, this means you want to find some way to use magic so that you can keep on doing what you've been doing, but get a different result. That's not going to happen. The universe is not going to change its mind and suddenly start paying you to do what you love. So you have a choice: learn to be comfortable with poverty, or change your life and prioritize making money the way you now prioritize art.

You can't have it both ways. If you want to be an artist, you're going to be like most artists, i.e., poor. If you don't want to be poor, you have to do something about it. Take your pick.

replies.title: Re: A spot of magical frustration
replies.content:
Okay, in that case I'm going to focus on the other stuff - books, publishing, invention, etc., since those are what L. George seems to suggest; I'm not devoid of ideas for those. Maybe I'll even find an institution I can work with.

Perhaps the true purpose with magic is to start on some sort of spiritual path. George even appears to hint at this - that with certain troublesome aspects such studies begin in an attempt to deal with difficulties.

replies.title: Re: A spot of magical frustration
replies.content: That sounds like a very constructive approach.

comments.title: Differing experiences on US rivers
comments.content:
For the past week my husband and I were on a steamboat cruising on the Ohio River from Cincinnati to its confluence with the Mississippi River and then upriver to St. Louis where we live. Because we both wanted to experience as much of the river and its surroundings as possible, we didn't attend any of the musical entertainments, instead sitting on one of the decks so we could watch and listen to and smell the rivers and the landscape around them.

As we approached the confluence of the two rivers, the so-called Riverlorian told us that there was a tradition (among boaters, I suppose, though he didn't specify and I didn't ask) of toasting the confluence with champagne and tossing a coin into the confluence while making a wish. I was a bit taken aback with the making-a-wish part since it was impromptu and I didn't want to upset any other-than-human beings with an inappropriate wish. So I settled on a general blessing for the coin toss.

Interestingly, during the time we spent on the Mississippi, I had a subtle but persistent sense of what I can best describe as the spirit of the river. Nothing changed in what I did while we were cruising up the Mississippi versus cruising down the Ohio, yet I did not notice any sense of the spirit of the Ohio, but did of the spirit of the Mississippi. Since a comment on an MM some months back described a sense that person had of a spirit of the Ohio River, I don't believe it was because the spirit of the Ohio isn't present.

Here are my questions.

1. Could the coin toss, toast, and blessing have had something to do with the perception I had? Perhaps it opened me to experience a subtle realm.

2. Might it have been because I'm more attuned to the Mississippi River than to the Ohio River? I've lived in the St. Louis area for 35 years and within 5 miles of the river for the last 30 of these. Furthermore, I had a strong intuitive sense that I belong here when I interviewed for the job I came here to work. I've neither lived near the Ohio River nor had an intuitive sense of being at home in the lands around it.

3. Could it have had something to do with the way people have treated each river? While the upper Mississippi is dammed to the point of being a series of pools, from St. Louis downriver the river runs free. I don't have a strong enough sense of the history of the Ohio to know how it may respond to human treatment, but the lower Mississippi seems to know it's in charge, whatever we may think. Maybe other readers with a sense of the Ohio might respond.

4a. Do I surmise correctly that praying to the spirit of the Mississippi and asking what sort of offering it might enjoy is in order? I know it doesn't need me, but I feel gratitude toward it for the sense.

4b. Should I do a divination first regarding 4a? I've been praying for a few years but it still feels awkward, and I don't want to do the wrong thing. The river has had more than enough maltreatment from humans without any further contribution from me.

Thank you for your response!

replies.title: Re: Differing experiences on US rivers
replies.content: This is fascinating, and it's completely outside my experience. You're likely to be able to answer these questions better than I am! In your place, though, I'd definitely do a divination and, if it's favorable, make offerings.

comments.title: "Star Maker"
comments.content:
Your discussion of science fiction over on the other blog reminded me of something interesting.

One of the most underappreciated writers of science fiction from the 1930s is Olaf Stapledon - a number of the major science fiction authors of the third quarter of the 20th century or so list him as one of their biggest influences. He is probably best known for "Last and First Men", a history of the human species from the 30s to the last member of the 18th successive human species dying in the planet Neptune 4 billion years from now, and "Star Maker", which can only be described as 'a 100 billion year long history of minds coming to terms with the fact that they exist' in which the events of Last and First Men only warrant a single paragraph. He has single sentences in those stories that lesser writers would use as the themes of entire books. The Martians and Venusians of the first book and the huge variety of alien entities in the latter book put almost all aliens in fiction since to shame.

Anyway, it occurrs to me that a good quantity of both of those books takes place via, for lack of better terms, scrying and astral projection. In Last and First Men, the book itself is presented as an account written by one of the 18th men, mentally transmitted into the mind of a modern author from one of their final generation after they took up a project of mental-time-travel to understand the entire long history of the various human species. In Star Maker, the point-of-view character finds themselves looking up at the sky and wondering what and who might be up there, and finds themselves drawn, disembodied, up from the Earth, and drawn through both time and space to a world on which beings very much like people live. As he sees more and more beings across time and space, the kinds of beings he can understand and identify with expands and he finds himself (and other fellow traveling consciousnesses) able to pick out of all of spacetime ever more inhuman and different beings and visit and experience and empathize with and understand them. It ends with great minds at the end of time learning from these mental travelers in their attempts to understand 'the Star Maker' and letting them take a tiny fraction of what they found back with them to their mundane existence.

Do you (or anyone else) know if Stapledon was directly influenced by sources in occult spheres, or if he likely just picked it up in the cultural milieu of philosophy professorship between the world wars in England?

replies.title: Re: "Star Maker"
replies.content: Fascinating. I have no idea, but it sounds like a very interesting research project.

replies.content:
How to make a novena:

A novena is a prayer addressed to a specific saint or deity for a particular intention for a specific amount of time, usually 9 days, although they can go on longer, sometimes decades, as did the novena for the conversion of Russia after the Communists took over. These long ones are called “perpetual novenas” and go until the intention is granted or until the answer is a definite No.

A group perpetual novena starts with determining the intention and what day of the week you’re going to pray it on. We have that part done! 👍. We Roman Catholics have numerous pre-written novenas we can use, we just plug in the intention. I doubt God would object if you look some up to see if they can be adapted to your own method of prayer. If you want to write your own, a novena prayer generally has 3 elements. There’s the intention. There’s asking for a virtue or virtues, usually one particularly associated with whomever your novena is addressed to. There’s thanksgiving, even if the intention hasn’t (yet) been granted, for good manners. It’s like calling your neighbor up and saying “Hey, Bob, can you help me trim my tree branches this weekend? Okay, thanks, bye.” Then when Bob helps you thank him again.

And that’s how it’s done! I suggest we tie it to Magic Monday to help us remember.

1) I heard once that swapping out terminology or symbols in magical workings (e.g., paganizing some of the more Christian rituals, for instance) can cause the ritual to lose its effect, or even backfire. Is this something you've experienced? Are there specific “dos and don'ts” for this sort of thing?

2) Something I see a lot of from polytheists (especially those of the Celtic variety) is the worship of gods that, according to the scholarly consensus, were almost certainly literary inventions of one scribe or another, and not actual deities that pre-Christian peoples would have ever heard of (a close-to-home example would be the Druidic god Hu Gadarn, but bigger players like Danu and Macha would also qualify as questionable). I don't doubt the reported efficacy of invoking these deities in magical work, nor do I doubt the experiences of their followers, but it raises the question: if these gods “don't exist” (at least from a historical/scholarly perspective), then who exactly is answering to these names?
